title: Distributional Sliced-Wasserstein and Applications to Generative Modeling

## DISTRIBUTIONAL SLICED-WASSERSTEIN AND APPLICATIONS TO GENERATIVE MODELING

## **Khai Nguyen**

VinAI Research, Vietnam v.khainb@vinai.io

## **Nhat Ho** _[∗]_

University of Texas, Austin VinAI Research, Vietnam minhnhat@utexas.edu

## **Tung Pham**

VinAI Research, Vietnam v.tungph4@vinai.io

## **Hung Bui**

VinAI Research, Vietnam v.hungbh1@vinai.io

## ABSTRACT

Sliced-Wasserstein distance (SW) and its variant, Max Sliced-Wasserstein distance (Max-SW), have been used widely in the recent years due to their fast computation and scalability even when the probability measures lie in a very high dimensional space. However, SW requires many unnecessary projection samples to approximate its value while Max-SW only uses the most important projection, which ignores the information of other useful directions. In order to account for these weaknesses, we propose a novel distance, named _Distributional Sliced-Wasserstein_ distance (DSW), that finds an _optimal_ distribution over projections that can balance between exploring distinctive projecting directions and the informativeness of projections themselves. We show that the DSW is a generalization of Max-SW, and it can be computed efficiently by searching for the optimal push-forward measure over a set of probability measures over the unit sphere satisfying certain regularizing constraints that favor distinct directions. Finally, we conduct extensive experiments with large-scale datasets to demonstrate the favorable performances of the proposed distances over the previous sliced-based distances in generative modeling applications.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Optimal transport (OT) is a classical problem in mathematics and operation research. Due to its appealing theoretical properties and flexibility in practical applications, it has recently become an important tool in the machine learning and statistics community; see for example, (Courty et al., 2017; Arjovsky et al., 2017; Tolstikhin et al., 2018; Gulrajani et al., 2017) and references therein. The main usage of OT is to provide a distance named Wasserstein distance, to measure the discrepancy between two probability distributions. However, that distance suffers from expensive computational complexity, which is the main obstacle to using OT in practical applications.

There have been two main approaches to overcome the high computational complexity problem: either approximate the value of OT or apply the OT adaptively to specific situations. The first approach was initiated by (Cuturi, 2013) using an entropic regularizer to speed up the computation of the OT (Sinkhorn, 1967; Knight, 2008). The entropic regularization approach has demonstrated its usefulness in several application domains (Courty et al., 2014; Genevay et al., 2018; Bunne et al., 2019). Along this direction, several works proposed efficient algorithms for solving the entropic OT (Altschuler et al., 2017; Lin et al., 2019b;a) as well as methods to stabilize these algorithms (Chizat et al., 2018; Peyré & Cuturi, 2019; Chizat et al., 2018; Schmitzer, 2019). However, these algorithms have complexities of the order _O_ ( _k_[2] ), where _k_ is the number of supports. It is expensive when we need to compute the OT repeatedly, especially in learning the data distribution.

The second approach, known as "slicing", takes a rather different perspective. It leverages two key ideas: the OT closed-form expression for two distributions in one-dimensional space, and the transformation of a distribution into a set of projected one-dimensional distributions by the Radon transform (RT) (Helgason, 2010). The popular proposal along this direction is Sliced-Wasserstein (SW) distance (Bonneel et al., 2015), which samples the projecting directions uniformly over a unit sphere in the data ambient space and takes the expectation of the resulting one-dimensional OT distance. The SW distance hence requires a significantly lower computation cost than the original Wasserstein distance and is more scalable than the first approach. Due to its solid statistical guarantees and efficient computation, the SW distance has been successfully applied to a variety of practical tasks (Deshpande et al., 2018; Liutkus et al., 2019; Kolouri et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2019; Deshpande et al., 2019) where it has been shown to have comparative performances to other distances and divergences between probability distributions. However, there is an inevitable bottleneck of computing the SW distance. Specifically, the expectation with respect to the uniform distribution of projections in SW is intractable to compute; therefore, the Monte Carlo method is employed to approximate it. Nevertheless, drawing from a uniform distribution of directions in high-dimension can result in an overwhelming number of irrelevant directions, especially when the actual data lies in a low-dimensional manifold. Hence, SW typically needs to have a large number of samples to yield an accurate estimation of the discrepancy. Alternatively, in the other extreme, Max Sliced-Wasserstein (Max-SW) distance (Deshpande et al., 2019) uses only one important direction to distinguish the probability distributions. However, other potentially relevant directions are ignored in Max-SW. Therefore, Max-SW can miss some important differences between the two distributions in high dimension. We note that the linear projections in the Radon transform can be replaced by non-linear projections resulting in the generalized sliced-Wasserstein distance and its variants (Beylkin, 1984; Kolouri et al., 2019).

Apart from these main directions, there are also few proposals that try either to modify them or to combine the advantages of the above-mentioned approaches. In particular, Paty & Cuturi (2019) extended the idea of the max-sliced distance to the max-subspace distance by considering finding an optimal orthogonal subspace. However, this approach is computationally expensive, since it could not exploit the closed-form of the one-dimensional Wasserstein distance. Another approach named the Projected Wasserstein distance (PWD), which was proposed in (Rowland et al., 2019), uses sliced decomposition to find multiple one-dimension optimal transport maps. Then, it computes the average cost of those maps equally in the original dimension.

**Our contributions.** Our paper also follows the slicing approach. However, we address key friction in this general line of work: how to obtain a relatively small number of slices simultaneously to maintain the computational efficiency, but at the same time, cover the major differences between two high-dimensional distributions. We take a probabilistic view of slicing by using a probability measure on the unit sphere to represent how important each direction is. From this viewpoint, SW uses the uniform distribution while Max-SW searches for the best delta-Dirac distribution over the projections, both can be considered as special cases. In this paper, we propose to search for an optimal distribution of important directions. We regularize this distribution such that it prefers directions that are far away from one another, hence encouraging an efficient exploration of the space of directions. In the case of no regularization, our proposed method recovers max-(generalized) SW as a special case. In summary, our main contributions are two-fold:

1. First, we introduce a novel distance, named _Distributional Sliced-Wasserstein distance_ (DSW), to account for the issues of previous sliced distances. Our main idea is to search for not just a single most important projection, but an _optimal_ distribution over projections that could balance between an expansion of the area around important projections and the informativeness of projections themselves, i.e., how well they can distinguish the two target probability measures. We show that DSW is a proper metric in the probability space and possesses appealing statistical and computational properties as the previous sliced distances.

2. Second, we apply the DSW distance to generative modeling tasks based on the generative adversarial framework. The extensive experiments on real and large-scale datasets show that DSW distance significantly outperforms the SW and Max-SW distances under similar computational time on these tasks. Furthermore, the DSW distance helps model distribution converge to the data distribution faster and provides more realistic generated images than the SW and Max-SW distances.

**Organization.** The remainder of the paper is organized as follows. In Section 2, we provide backgrounds for Wasserstein distance and its slice-based versions. In Section 3, we propose distributional (generalized) sliced-Wasserstein distance and analyze some of its theoretical properties. Section 4 includes extensive experiment results followed by discussions in Section 5. Finally, we defer the proofs of key results and extra materials in the Appendices.

However, important directions are not distributed uniformly over the sphere. Thus, this approach will draw potentially many unimportant projections that are not only expensive but also greatly reduce the effect of the SW distance.

## 2.4 MAX (GENERALIZED) SLICED-WASSERSTEIN DISTANCES

An approach to using only informative directions is to simply take the best slice in discriminating two given probability distributions. That distance is max sliced-Wasserstein distance (Max-SW) (Deshpande et al., 2019), which is given by max _SWp_ ( _µ, ν_ ) := max _θ∈_ S _d−_ 1 _Wp_ ( _RIµ_ ( _·, θ_ ) _, RIν_ ( _·, θ_ )). By combining this idea with non-linear projections from generalized Radon transform, we obtain max generalized sliced-Wasserstein distance (Max-GSW) (Kolouri et al., 2019). The formal definition of that distance is: max _GSWp_ ( _µ, ν_ ) := max _θ∈_ Ω _θ Wp_ ( _GIµ_ ( _·, θ_ ) _, GIν_ ( _·, θ_ )). The (generalized) MaxSW distances focus on finding only the most important direction. Meanwhile, other informative directions play no role in the distance. Therefore, (generalized) Max-SW distances can ignore useful information about the structure of high dimensional probability measures.

## 3 DISTRIBUTIONAL SLICED-WASSERSTEIN DISTANCE

With the aim of improving the limitations of the previous sliced distances, we propose a novel distance, named _Distributional Sliced-Wasserstein distance_ (DSW), that can search for not just a single but a distribution of important directions on the unit sphere. We prove that it is a well-defined metric and discuss its connection to the existing sliced-based distances in Section 3.1. Then, we provide a procedure to approximate DSW based on its dual form in Section 3.2.

## 3.1 DEFINITION AND METRICITY

We first start with a definition of distributional sliced-Wasserstein distance. We say _C >_ 0 _admissible_ if the set M _C_ of probability measures _σ_ on S _[d][−]_[1] satisfying E _θ,θ′∼σ_ � _|θ[⊤] θ[′] |_ � _≤ C_ is not empty.

**Definition 1.** _Given two probability measures µ and ν on_ R _[d] with finite p-th moments where p ≥_ 1 _and an admissible regularizing constant C >_ 0 _. The_ distributional sliced-Wasserstein distance _(DSW) of order p between µ and ν is given by:_

_where R is the Radon transform operator._

The DSW aims to find the optimal probability measure of slices on the unit sphere S _[d][−]_[1] . Note that, the Max-SW distance is equivalent to searching for the best Dirac measure on a single point in S _[d][−]_[1] , which puts all weights in only one direction. Meanwhile, the uniform measure in the formulation of SW distance distributes the same weights in all directions. Indeed, the uniform and Dirac measures are two special cases, because they view that either all directions are equally important or only one direction is important. That view is too restricted if the data actually lie on low dimensional space. Thus, we aim to find a probability measure which concentrates only on areas around important directions. Furthermore, we do not want these directions to lie in only one small area, because under the orthogonal projection of RT, their corresponding one-dimensional distributions will become similar. In order to achieve this, we search for an optimal measure _σ_ that satisfies the regularization constraint E _θ,θ[′] ∼σ_ [�� _θ⊤θ′_[�] �] _≤ C_ . By Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, _C_ is no greater than 1, thus M1 contains all probability measures on the unit sphere. Optimizing over M1 simply returns the best Dirac measure corresponding to the Max-SW distance. When _C_ is small, the constraint forces the measure _σ_ to distribute more weights to directions that are far from each other (in terms of their angles). Thus, a small appropriate value of _C_ will help to balance between the distinctiveness and informativeness of these targeted directions. For further discussion about _C_ , see Appendix B.1. Next, we show that DSW is a well-defined metric on the probability space. **Theorem 1.** _For any p ≥_ 1 _and admissible C >_ 0 _, DSWp_ ( _·, ·_ ; _C_ ) _is a well-defined metric in the space of Borel probability measures with finite p-th moment. In particular, it is non-negative, symmetric, identity, and satisfies the triangle inequality._

The proof of Theorem 2 is in Appendix A.2. As a consequence of Theorem 2, the statistical error of estimating the unknown distribution based on the empirical distribution of _n_ i.i.d data under DSW distance is _Cd · n[−]_[1] _[/]_[2] with high probability where _Cd_ is some universal constant depending on dimension _d_ (see Appendix B.3). Therefore, as other sliced-based Wasserstein distances, the DSW distance does not suffer from the curse of dimensionality.

**Finding the optimal** _f_ **:** We parameterize _f_ in the dual form (2) by using a deep neural network with parameter _φ_ , defined as _fφ_ . Then, we estimate the gradient of the objective function DS( _fφ_ ) in equation (2) with respect to _φ_ and use stochastic gradient ascent algorithm to update _φ_ . Since there are expectations over uniform distribution in the gradient of DS( _fφ_ ), we use the Monte Carlo method to approximate these expectations. Note that, we can use the fixed point from the stochastic ascent algorithm to approximate the dual value of DSW in equation (2). A detailed argument for this point is in Appendix B.2. Finally, in generative model applications with DSW being the loss function, we only need to use the gradient of the function DS( _._ ) to update the parameters of interest. Therefore, we can treat _λC_ as a regularized parameter and tune it to find suitable value in these applications.

## 4 EXPERIMENTS

In this section, we conduct extensive experiments comparing the performance in both generative quality and computational speed of the proposed DSW distance with other sliced-based distances, namely the SW, Max-SW, Max-GSW-NN (Kolouri et al., 2019) and projected robust subspace Wasserstein (PRW) (Paty & Cuturi, 2019; Lin et al., 2020) using the minimum expected distance estimator (MEDE) (Bernton et al., 2019) on MNIST (LeCun et al., 1998), CIFAR10 (Krizhevsky, 2009), CelebA (Liu et al., 2015) and LSUN (Yu et al., 2015) datasets. The details of the MEDE framework are described in Appendix D. We would like to note that the wall-clock timing of different methods may be subject to the differences in the hyperparameter settings and software implementations of different methods. On MNIST dataset, we train generative models with different distances and then evaluate their performances by comparing Wasserstein-2 distances between 10000 random generated images and all images from the MNIST test set. Due to the very large size of other datasets, e.g., 3 million images in LSUN, it is expensive to compute empirical Wasserstein-2 distance as its complexity is of order _O_ ( _k_[2] log _k_ ) where _k_ is the number of support points. Therefore, after we train generative models, we use FID score (Heusel et al., 2017) to evaluate the generative quality of these generators. The FID score is calculated from 10000 random generated images and all training samples using precomputed statistics in (Heusel et al., 2017). Finally, for _λC_ in DSW (see Definition 2), it is chosen in the set _{_ 1 _,_ 10 _,_ 100 _,_ 1000 _}_ such that its Wasserstein-2 (FID score) (between 10000 random generated images and all images from corresponding validation set) is the lowest among the four values. Detailed experiment settings are in Appendix G. Finally, we also apply the DSW into color transfer task (Rabin et al., 2010; 2014; Bonneel et al., 2015; Perrot et al., 2016) in Appendix F, where we find that DSW also performs better than SW and Max-SW in this task.

Then we measure the computational time per minibatch and the Wasserstein-2 score of the learned generators for each _N_ . We plot the Wasserstein-2 score and computational time of Max-SW and Max-GSW-NN in their standard settings (Kolouri et al., 2019). Except for the regime with very fast but low-quality learned models, DSW is better than all the existing slice-based baselines in terms of both model quality and computational speed. Moreover, DSW can learn good models with very few projections, e.g., DSW-10 achieves better model quality than Max-GSW-NN and Max-SW and is one order-of-magnitude faster than these sliced distances. Finally, with a similar computational time, a learned generator by DSW has the Wasserstein-2 score that is roughly 10% lower than the one got from SW. For the qualitative comparison between these distances, we show random generated images from their generative models in Figure 7 in Appendix E.1. We observe that generated images from DSW are sharper and easier to classify into numbers than those from other baseline distances.

**Comparison with projected robust subspace Wasserstein (PRW) and Wasserstein distance:** In Figure 2(a), we plot the Wasserstein-2 score and computational time of Wasserstein distance (WD) and PRW, where the subspace dimension of PRW varies in the range _{_ 2 _,_ 5 _,_ 10 _,_ 50 _}_ . PRW is able to improve upon the model quality of slice-based methods including DSW, however at the cost of being an order of magnitude slower than DSW with 10 projections (DSW-10). We observe that DSW-10 obtains a better Wasserstein-2 score than PRW with 5-dimensional subspace, while its corresponding computational time is 30 times faster than that of PRW-5. Using 50 dimension, PRW’s Wasserstein-2 score improves about 29% to that of DSW-10 but the computational cost is also around 40 times slower. The model trained by WD gives good Wasserstein-2 score; however, it is computational expensive (about 40 times slower than DSW-10). The main computational advantage of DSW comes from the exact calculation of Wasserstein distance in one-dimension. The visual comparison between PRW, WD and DSW based on their generated images is in Figure 12 in Appendix E.2.

**Convergence behavior:** Figure 2(b) shows that DSW learns better models at a faster speed of convergence than other baseline distances with a very small number of projections, e.g., DSW-10 is the second lowest curve compared to curves from other sliced-based distances.

**Scalability over sample size of minibatch:** Results in Figure 2(c) show that DSW has a computational complexity of the order _O_ ( _k_ log _k_ ), which is similar to those of other sliced-based distances, where _k_ is the number of samples per batch.

**Effect of the regularization parameter** _λC_ **:** For each value of _λC ∈{_ 1 _,_ 10 _,_ 100 _,_ 1000 _}_ , we find the optimal distribution _σ_ of DSW with _N_ = 10 projections, and then calculate _AN_ = _N_ 1[2] � _Ni,j_ =1 _[|][θ] i[⊤][θ][j][|]_[,] an approximation of the regularized term E _θ,θ′∼σ_ � _|θ[⊤] θ[′] |_ � in the dual form of DSW in equation (2), where _{θi}[N] i_ =1 _[∼][σ]_[.][The results are shown in Figure 2(d).][We observe that when] _[ λ][C]_[increases,] _[ A][N]_ goes down. **Additional experiments:** We also investigate how the number of gradient-steps used for updating distribution of directions _σ_ , and how the size of minibatches affects the quality of DSW (see Appendix E.1). The results show that an increasing number of gradient steps to update _σ_ leads to better performance of DSW but also slows down the computation speed. Furthermore, we carry out experiments with DGSW, an extension of DSW to non-linear projections, and test the new proposed distances in training encoder-generator models on MNIST using joint contrastive inference (JCI) in Appendices E.1 and E.3. The description of these models is in Appendix D.

## 4.2 RESULTS ON LARGE-SCALE DATASETS

Next, we conduct large-scale experiments on a range of more realistic image datasets. We train generative models using CIFAR10, CelebA, and LSUN datasets (all these datasets are rescaled to 64x64 resolution). When working with high dimensional distributions, Deshpande et al. (2018) proposed a trick to improve the quality of the generator by learning a feature function which maps data to a new feature space that is more manageable in size. When the feature function is fixed, the generator is trained to match the distribution of features. When the generator is fixed, the feature function tries to tease apart the data empirical features from the generated feature distribution. For the experiments in this section, we use the same technique with DSW and all other baseline distances.

We compare DSW with SW, Max-SW, and Max-GSW-NN in both generative quality (FID score) and computational time in Figure 3. We could not compare DSW with PRW on the large-scale datasets since PRW is computationally expensive to train to obtain good generated images. On CelebA and CIFAR10, we let _N_ , the number of projections of both DSW and SW, vary in the set _{_ 10[2] _,_ 5 _×_ 10[2] _,_ 10[3] _,_ 5 _×_ 10[3] _,_ 10[4] _}_ . For LSUN, since it takes considerably longer time to train each model, we only vary _N_ in the set _{_ 10[2] _,_ 10[4] _}_ . On all these large datasets, DSW outperforms all the other baselines in both FID score of the learned model and computational efficiency. The gap of FID scores between DSW and other methods is especially large on CIFAR10 and LSUN. For example, on CIFAR10, with the same computational time, FID scores of DSW are always lower than those of SW about 20 units. On LSUN, with 100 projections, DSW can achieve an FID score of 46 while SW with 10000 projections still has a worse FID score of over 60. It is interesting to note that on these high-dimensional datasets, Max-SW performs rather poorly: it obtains the highest FID scores among all distances while requires heavy computation. Max-GSW-NN has better FID scores than (Max)-SW; however, it is still worse than DSW and while being slower. This is consistent with the intuition that as the number of dimension of the data grows, the use of a single important slice in Max-SW becomes a less efficient approximation. DSW, on the other hand, is able to make use of more important slices, and at the same time avoids SW’s inefficiency of uniform slice-sampling.

Generated images from CelebA, CIFAR10 and LSUN are deferred to Appendix E.1. Comparing to other sliced-based Wasserstein distances, generated samples obtained from the DSW’s generative model are also more visually realistic. Further experiments to compare DGSW with GSW, Max-GSW, and Max-GSW-NN are also given in the Appendix E.1. Based on these experiments, we can conclude that the distributional approach also improves the generative quality of non-linear slicing distances.

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## 5 CONCLUSION

In this paper, we have presented the novel distributional sliced-Wasserstein (DSW) distances between two probability measures. Our main idea is to search for the best distribution of important directions while regularizing towards orthogonal directions. We prove that they are well-defined metrics and provide their theoretical and computational properties. We compare our proposed distances to other sliced-based distances in a variety of generative modeling tasks, including estimating generative models and jointly estimating both generators and inference models. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our new distances yield significantly better models and convergence behaviors during training than the previous sliced-based distances. One important future direction is to investigate theoretically the optimal choice of the regularization parameter _λC_ such that the DSW distance can capture all the important directions that can distinguish two target probability measures well.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
The paper introduces the Distributional Sliced-Wasserstein (DSW) distance, which replaces the uniform sampling of projections in standard Sliced-Wasserstein (SW) with an optimized probability measure over the projection space, regularized to encourage diversity among directions. The authors demonstrate that DSW provides a more efficient and discriminative metric for generative modeling compared to existing sliced-based variants.

The paper makes a clear and well-motivated contribution to the field of optimal transport-based generative modeling. The theoretical derivation of DSW as a metric is sound, and the empirical results across several datasets strongly support the claim that optimizing the distribution of projections leads to better generative quality and training efficiency. It is a strong piece of work that addresses a known bottleneck in sliced-Wasserstein methods.

Overview issues:

- Unclear sensitivity and selection of the regularization parameter λ_C: The dual form of DSW in Definition 2 depends heavily on the regularization parameter λ_C, which controls the trade-off between the informativeness of projections and their diversity. While the authors suggest tuning λ_C in the set {1, 10, 100, 1000}, they provide little theoretical guidance on how this parameter scales with data dimension or the complexity of the target distribution. Without a principled way to choose λ_C, the method risks being overly sensitive to hyperparameter tuning in practical applications. A more thorough analysis of the relationship between λ_C, the geometry of the data manifold, and the resulting distribution of projections is necessary to ensure the method's reliability.

- Lack of empirical validation for the convergence of the optimal measure: The methodology relies on a stochastic gradient ascent process to find the optimal distribution of projections, parameterized by a neural network f_φ. However, the paper does not provide evidence regarding the stability or convergence of this inner optimization loop during the training of the generator. If the distribution of projections does not converge or oscillates significantly, the gradient estimates for the generator could become noisy and hinder training. Including a diagnostic plot showing the stability of the inner optimization or the variance of the gradient estimates across training iterations would strengthen the claims regarding the method's robustness.

- Ambiguity in the definition of the admissible set M_C: Theorem 1 establishes that DSW is a well-defined metric, but the proof relies on the assumption that the set M_C is non-empty for a given admissible C. While the paper notes that C <= 1 satisfies this, the practical implications of choosing C for different dimensions are not fully explored. The paper would benefit from a more explicit discussion on how the choice of C affects the metric's properties, particularly in high-dimensional settings where the volume of the unit sphere is large. Clarifying the constraints on C would help users understand the limitations of the metric in various data regimes.

- Comparison with non-sliced baseline methods: While the paper compares DSW against several sliced-based variants (SW, Max-SW, Max-GSW-NN), it lacks a comparison against non-sliced methods that also scale to high dimensions, such as entropic Wasserstein or other kernel-based distribution distances. While the authors mention that standard Wasserstein is computationally expensive, showing where DSW sits on the Pareto frontier relative to these other approaches would provide a more complete picture of its utility. This would clarify whether the performance gains are due strictly to the distributional slicing approach or if they are competitive with broader classes of distribution-matching losses.

- Lack of a concrete counterexample where SW or Max-SW fails: The paper claims that DSW is superior because SW uses too many irrelevant directions and Max-SW uses too few, but it lacks a formal or synthetic example where these specific failure modes are isolated and quantified. A simple, low-dimensional synthetic experiment—such as two Gaussian mixtures where the modes are separated along specific non-orthogonal axes—would demonstrate exactly how the 'distributional' approach succeeds where the others fail. Without this, the reader must take the authors' intuition on faith rather than observing the mechanism in action. I suggest computing the DSW, SW, and Max-SW distances for a two-component Gaussian mixture model in 10-dimensional space where the means are separated by a specific, sparse set of directions, and demonstrating that DSW correctly identifies the informative subspace while the others do not.

- Missing analysis of the inner loop's impact on generator stability: The DSW loss relies on an inner optimization loop (the search for the optimal distribution of projections) that is nested within the outer training loop of the generative model. The paper does not analyze the potential for the inner optimization to become a moving target, which could induce significant variance or instability in the generator's gradient updates. To address this, the authors should provide a simulation or diagnostic plot that tracks the variance of the gradient of the DSW loss with respect to the generator parameters over the course of training. Showing that the inner loop converges sufficiently fast relative to the outer loop is necessary to justify the claim that DSW is a stable and efficient loss function for GAN training.

Recommendation: minor revision

Revision targets:

- Provide a more detailed analysis or heuristic for selecting the regularization parameter λ_C based on data characteristics.

- Include a diagnostic analysis showing the stability of the inner loop optimization during generative model training.

- Expand the discussion on the constraints of the admissible set M_C and how the choice of C influences the metric properties in practice.

- Add a brief comparison or discussion regarding the performance of DSW against non-sliced methods to better contextualize the results.

comments.explanation: The claim that optimizing over M_1 returns the Max-SW distance is incorrect. Since M_1 contains all probability measures on the unit sphere, the supremum over M_1 includes the uniform measure (which yields the standard SW distance) and is therefore at least as large as the SW distance. coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Coarse (deepseek-v4-flash)
coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes a new distance metric, Distributional Sliced-Wasserstein (DSW), for generative modeling, aiming to balance the trade-offs between uniform and max slicing. While the idea is interesting and the empirical results on several datasets are promising, several major concerns need to be addressed regarding the theoretical justification, computational assumptions, and empirical rigor.

The paper introduces a conceptually appealing generalization of sliced-Wasserstein distances that learns a distribution over slicing directions. The empirical results on MNIST, CIFAR10, CelebA, and LSUN show consistent improvements over SW and Max-SW in terms of FID and computational efficiency. However, the paper has significant gaps in its theoretical analysis (e.g., the metricity proof, the dual formulation, and the behavior of the regularization), and the empirical evaluation lacks crucial details about training stability and sensitivity to hyperparameters. The contribution is incremental rather than transformative, and the paper would benefit from a more thorough investigation of the method's limitations.

Overview issues:

- Metricity proof is incomplete and relies on unverified assumptions: Theorem 1 claims that DSW is a metric, but the proof in Appendix A.1 (not fully visible in the provided text) must carefully handle the case when the supremum over σ is not attained. The definition of 'admissible C' (set M_C non-empty) is vague; for C very small, M_C may contain only measures that put mass on orthogonal directions, but it is not shown that such measures exist for all C > 0. Moreover, the triangle inequality proof likely relies on the fact that the Wasserstein distance satisfies it for each fixed slice, but the supremum over σ may break the triangle inequality unless the same σ works for all pairs. The paper does not address this subtlety. The authors should provide a rigorous proof that the supremum over σ preserves the triangle inequality, or clarify under what conditions it does.

- The dual formulation and the role of λ_C are not properly justified: Definition 2 introduces a dual form of DSW with a Lagrange multiplier λ_C, but the derivation is not provided. The paper states that DSW ≥ DSW* by Lagrangian duality, but this inequality is reversed in standard duality theory (the primal is a constrained maximization, so the Lagrangian gives an upper bound, not a lower bound). The text says 'DSW_p ≥ DSW*_p', which is the opposite of what one would expect. This suggests a fundamental misunderstanding or misstatement. The authors should clarify the primal-dual relationship and provide a proper derivation. Additionally, the claim that λ_C can be treated as a tunable hyperparameter without justification of how it relates to C undermines the theoretical contribution.

- Computational tractability and scalability are not adequately demonstrated: The paper claims DSW is computationally efficient, but the optimization over f (a neural network) introduces a nested optimization loop that could be expensive. The experiments use only up to 10,000 projections for SW and DSW, but the cost of updating the slice distribution network is not reported separately. For high-dimensional data like LSUN (64x64 images), the Radon transform of each slice requires O(k log k) operations, but the additional cost of the neural network forward and backward passes for the slice distribution is not quantified. The paper should report wall-clock time breakdowns and show how the method scales to larger datasets (e.g., ImageNet 128x128). Without this, the claim of 'similar computational complexity to SW' is misleading.

- The regularization constraint and its effect on the slice distribution are poorly understood: The constraint E[|θ^T θ'|] ≤ C is meant to encourage diversity among slices, but its effect is not analyzed. The paper shows empirically that larger λ_C (smaller C) leads to more orthogonal directions, but there is no theoretical guarantee that the optimal σ under this constraint actually captures informative directions. It is possible that the constraint forces the distribution to spread mass over many directions, including irrelevant ones, reducing the discriminative power. The authors should provide a more rigorous analysis of the trade-off between diversity and informativeness, perhaps using a toy example where the optimal σ is known. The current empirical illustration with two Gaussians is insufficient.

- Empirical evaluation lacks critical baselines and ablation studies: The paper compares DSW only against SW, Max-SW, Max-GSW-NN, and PRW. Missing baselines include: (a) the standard Wasserstein GAN with gradient penalty (WGAN-GP), which is a strong baseline for generative modeling; (b) the Sinkhorn distance (entropic OT), which is another popular OT-based method; (c) a variant of SW with importance sampling or adaptive slicing (e.g., from the literature). Additionally, the paper does not ablate the effect of the number of gradient steps for updating the slice distribution (mentioned only briefly in Appendix E.1). The FID scores on CIFAR10 (e.g., DSW ~40-50) are much higher than state-of-the-art GANs (e.g., StyleGAN2 achieves FID < 10), suggesting that the method is not competitive with modern generative models. The paper should include a discussion of this gap and clarify that the comparison is only among sliced-Wasserstein methods.

- The paper overclaims the generality of DSW and its theoretical properties: The abstract and introduction claim that DSW 'is a proper metric' and 'possesses appealing statistical and computational properties as the previous sliced distances.' However, the statistical properties (e.g., sample complexity) are only briefly mentioned in Appendix B.3 with a claim of O(n^{-1/2}) rate, but no proof is provided. The computational properties are not rigorously analyzed; the paper only shows empirical scaling plots. The claim that DSW 'does not suffer from the curse of dimensionality' is misleading because the sample complexity bound depends on the dimension d through the constant C_d, which may be exponential in d. The authors should provide precise statements and proofs, or temper the claims.

- The connection to Max-GSW-NN is not clearly distinguished: In Section 3.2 and Appendix E.1, the paper notes that when the defining function in Max-GSW-NN is g(x,θ) = <x, f(θ)>, DSW differs from Max-GSW-NN. However, this comparison is not elaborated. The paper should explain how DSW's optimization over a distribution of slices is fundamentally different from learning a single nonlinear projection in Max-GSW-NN. Without this, a reader might wonder whether DSW is simply a regularized version of Max-GSW-NN. A clear theoretical or empirical distinction is needed.

- Missing demonstration that DSW is a proper metric: The paper claims DSW is a proper metric (Theorem 1), but the proof is not provided in the visible text. The definition of admissible C is vague, and it is not shown that the supremum over σ preserves the triangle inequality. A rigorous proof is needed, including a discussion of when the supremum is attained and how the constraint E[|θ^T θ'|] ≤ C ensures the triangle inequality. Without this, the metricity claim is unsubstantiated.

- Computational cost of optimizing the slice distribution is not quantified: The paper claims DSW has similar computational complexity to SW, but the nested optimization over the neural network f adds significant overhead. The wall-clock time breakdown (e.g., time for updating f vs. computing projections) is not reported. For high-dimensional data like LSUN, the cost of the neural network forward/backward passes should be quantified. Without this, the claim of computational efficiency is misleading.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Fix the dual formulation and the inequality direction; provide a proper derivation of the Lagrangian and clarify the relationship between λ_C and C.

- Provide a rigorous proof of metricity (Theorem 1) that addresses the triangle inequality and the existence of the supremum.

- Add a computational complexity analysis that separates the cost of updating the slice distribution from the projection cost, and report wall-clock time breakdowns for large-scale datasets.

- Include additional baselines (e.g., WGAN-GP, Sinkhorn) and ablation studies on the number of gradient steps for the slice distribution and the effect of λ_C on generative quality.

- Discuss the gap between DSW's FID scores and state-of-the-art GANs, and clarify that the comparison is limited to sliced-Wasserstein methods.

comments.title: Dual inequality direction is reversed
comments.quote: By the Lagrangian duality theory, $\text{DSW}_{p}(\mu,\nu;C)\geq\text{DSW}^{*}_{p}(\mu,\nu;C)$ for any $p\geq 1$ and admissible $C>0$.
comments.explanation: For a constrained maximization problem, the Lagrangian dual typically provides an upper bound on the primal optimum, not a lower bound. The primal problem in Definition 1 is a constrained maximization over σ in M_C. Standard Lagrangian duality for maximization problems yields DSW_p ≤ DSW*_p, not ≥. The authors should verify the direction of the inequality for their specific formulation. If the inequality is indeed reversed, the derivation of the dual form and the interpretation of λ_C need to be re-examined. Rewrite the sentence to correctly state the inequality after a proper derivation, or provide a justification for the claimed direction.

comments.title: Triangle inequality for DSW requires justification
comments.quote:
$C>0$, $\text{DSW}_{p}(\cdot,\cdot;C)$ is a well-defined metric in the space of Borel probability measures with finite $p$-th moment. In particular, it is non-negative, symmetric, identity, and satisfies the triangle inequality.

<
comments.explanation: The triangle inequality for DSW is not automatically inherited from the Wasserstein distance because the supremum over σ may use different σ for different pairs. The standard argument: for any μ,ν,ρ and any σ ∈ M_C, W_p(Rμ(·,θ),Rν(·,θ)) ≤ W_p(Rμ(·,θ),Rρ(·,θ)) + W_p(Rρ(·,θ),Rν(·,θ)). Taking expectation over θ∼σ and then p-th root gives DSW_p(μ,ν;C) ≤ DSW_p(μ,ρ;C) + DSW_p(ρ,ν;C) by Minkowski's inequality for integrals and the fact that sup_σ (A_σ + B_σ) ≤ sup_σ A_σ + sup_σ B_σ. This reasoning is standard but should be explicitly stated or referenced. The paper's claim that the proof is in Appendix A.1 is acceptable, but the visible text does not address the subtlety. Add a brief remark in the main text: 'The triangle inequality follows from the triangle inequality of W_p and Minkowski's inequality for integrals, together with the property sup_σ (A_σ + B_σ) ≤ sup_σ A_σ + sup_σ B_σ.'

comments.title: Reparametrization may not cover all measures
comments.quote: For reparametrizing the measure $\sigma$, we use a pushforward of uniform measure on the unit sphere through some measurable function $f$. In particular, let $f$ be a Borel measurable function from $\mathbb{S}^{d-1}$ to $\mathbb{S}^{d-1}$. For any Borel set $A\subset\mathbb{S}^{d-1}$, we define $\sigma(A)=\sigma^{d-1}(f^{-1}(A))$, where $\sigma^{d-1}$ is the uniform probability measure on $\mathbb{S}^{d-1}$.
comments.explanation: The pushforward construction σ = f_# σ^{d-1} only generates measures that are absolutely continuous with respect to the uniform measure on the sphere (since σ^{d-1} is non-atomic and f is measurable). However, the original optimization in Definition 2 is over all probability measures on S^{d-1}, including singular or discrete measures. The paper does not argue that the supremum over this restricted class equals the supremum over all measures. Without such an argument, the reparametrized dual form may be strictly smaller than the original DSW*, potentially affecting the validity of the approximation. The authors should either prove that the supremum over pushforward measures is dense in the space of all measures (e.g., in the weak topology) or restrict the claim to the class of measures that can be represented this way.

comments.title: Missing justification for λ_C existence
comments.quote: For any $p\geq 1$ and admissible $C>0$, there exists a non-negative constant $\lambda_{C}$ depending on $C$ such that the dual form of DSW distance takes the following
comments.explanation: The paper asserts the existence of λ_C without any derivation or reference to a specific Lagrangian duality theorem. The relationship between C and λ_C is not defined, and it is unclear how λ_C is computed from C. The subsequent discussion treats λ_C as a tunable parameter, but the claim that it exists for any admissible C is unsupported. The authors should either derive λ_C explicitly from the Lagrangian of the constrained problem in Definition 1 or cite a standard duality result that guarantees its existence. Add a derivation or reference to justify the existence of λ_C.

comments.title: FID evaluation uses training samples, not test samples
comments.quote: The FID score is calculated from 10000 random generated images and all training samples using precomputed statistics in (Heusel et al., 2017).
comments.explanation: Standard practice for FID evaluation is to compare generated images against the test set (or a held-out validation set), not the training set. Using training samples inflates FID scores because the generator may have memorized training data, and it conflates generalization quality with reconstruction of the training distribution. The paper should either justify why training samples are used or switch to the test set. If the test set is unavailable for LSUN, the authors should state this explicitly and note the limitation.

comments.title: Lambda_C selection uses the same metric as evaluation
comments.quote: lambda_C$  in DSW (see Definition 2), it is chosen in the set  $\{1,10,100,1000\}$  such that its Wasserstein-2 (FID score) (between 10000 random generated images and all images from corresponding validation set) is the lowest among the four values. Detai
comments.explanation: Selecting the hyperparameter λ_C by minimizing the evaluation metric (FID or Wasserstein-2) on the validation set introduces a form of overfitting to the validation set. The reported FID scores for DSW are therefore not a fair comparison with baselines whose hyperparameters were not tuned on the same validation metric. The authors should either use a separate validation split for tuning and report test-set FID, or use a principled criterion (e.g., cross-validation, marginal likelihood) that does not directly optimize the evaluation metric. At minimum, they should disclose that this tuning procedure was used and discuss its impact on the comparison.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about FID gap on CIFAR10
comments.quote: For example, on CIFAR10, with the same computational time, FID scores of DSW are always lower than those of SW about 20 units.
comments.explanation: The claim that DSW's FID scores are 'always lower than those of SW about 20 units' is not supported by any numerical data in this section. No FID values are reported for any method on CIFAR10, so the reader cannot verify this claim. The paper should include a table or explicit numbers (e.g., 'DSW achieved FID 45 vs. SW FID 65 at N=1000') to substantiate the magnitude of the improvement. Without such data, the claim is an unverifiable assertion.

comments.title: Missing FID values for LSUN comparison
comments.quote: On LSUN, with 100 projections, DSW can achieve an FID score of 46 while SW with 10000 projections still has a worse FID score of over 60.
comments.explanation: This is the only concrete FID number reported in the entire section, but it is presented without context. The reader does not know the FID of SW at 100 projections, nor the FID of Max-SW or Max-GSW-NN at any projection count. Without a full table or plot showing all methods at all N values, the reader cannot assess whether DSW's advantage is consistent or an artifact of a single comparison. The paper should provide a complete numerical comparison for all methods and projection counts on LSUN.

comments.title: Inconsistent number of projections for DSW in Figure 2(a) description
comments.explanation: The text states that for DSW, N is chosen from {1, 10, 10^2, 5×10^2, 10^3, 5×10^3}. However, in the caption of Figure 2(a), it says 'each dot of SW and DSW corresponds to the number of projections chosen in {1,10,10^2,5×10^2,10^3,5×10^3,10^4}.' This is a direct contradiction: the caption includes 10^4 for DSW, while the text excludes it. The authors should correct one of the statements to be consistent. If DSW was not tested with 10^4 projections, the caption should be updated; if it was, the text should include 10^4.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about DSW's O(k log k) complexity
comments.quote: esults in Figure 2(c) show that DSW has a computational complexity of the order  $\mathcal{O}(k\log k)$ , which is similar to those of other sliced-based distances, where  $k$  is the number of samples per batch.
comments.explanation: Figure 2(c) plots computational time vs. sample size on a log-log scale, but the text does not report the slope of the fitted line or the actual time values. A log-log plot with a slope of 1 would indicate O(k), not O(k log k). The difference between O(k) and O(k log k) is subtle and cannot be visually confirmed from a log-log plot without a reference line. Moreover, the computational complexity of DSW includes the cost of optimizing the slice distribution (e.g., gradient steps for the neural network f), which is not captured by the projection step alone. The claim that DSW has O(k log k) complexity is misleading because it ignores the overhead of the slice distribution optimization. The authors should either provide a rigorous complexity analysis that accounts for all components, or remove the claim and simply state that the empirical scaling is similar to SW.

comments.title: Cauchy-Schwarz bound justification is imprecise
comments.quote: C$. By Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, $C$ is no greater than $1$, thus $\mathbb{M}_{1}$ contains all probability measures on the unit sphere.
comments.explanation: The bound |θ^T θ'| ≤ 1 follows from the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality applied to the vectors θ and θ', not from an expectation inequality. The statement that M_1 contains all probability measures is correct because for any σ, E[|θ^T θ'|] ≤ 1, so every measure satisfies the constraint with C=1. The phrasing is slightly imprecise but not mathematically wrong. Rewrite as: 'Since |θ^T θ'| ≤ 1 for all θ,θ' ∈ S^{d-1}, we have E[|θ^T θ'|] ≤ 1 for any σ, so M_1 contains every probability measure on the sphere.'

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The manuscript proposes Distributional Sliced-Wasserstein (DSW), a distance that optimizes a probability measure over projection directions to balance directional exploration and informativeness. While the theoretical motivation is sound and experiments demonstrate competitive performance, several critical gaps remain regarding training dynamics, approximation guarantees, and computational accounting. Addressing these issues is necessary to fully validate the method's claims and ensure reproducibility.

The paper introduces a clean theoretical extension of sliced Wasserstein distances by searching for an optimal distribution over projection directions rather than relying on uniform sampling or a single maximum. The empirical results on MNIST, CIFAR10, CelebA, and LSUN clearly show improvements in both generation quality and speed compared to standard baselines. The core idea is well-motivated and addresses genuine inefficiencies in existing sliced approaches.

Overview issues:

- Unclear update schedule for the direction distribution optimization: Section 3.2 introduces a neural network $f_\phi$ to parameterize the optimal direction measure $\sigma$, but the paper never specifies how this network is updated during generative model training. The optimization of $\sigma$ creates a nested structure alongside the generator and discriminator parameters. Without a clear schedule, whether $f_\phi$ is updated every minibatch, every epoch, or kept fixed after initialization, the training dynamics remain ambiguous. This omission directly impacts reproducibility and makes it difficult to isolate why DSW converges faster in Figure 2(b). The authors should explicitly state the update frequency for $\sigma$ and provide an ablation study showing how different schedules affect training stability and final FID scores.

- Disconnect between exact metric properties and practical approximations: Theorem 1 and Theorem 2 establish that the exact DSW formulation is a well-defined metric and topologically equivalent to standard Wasserstein distances. Definition 2 and Equation 2, however, rely on a Lagrangian dual form and a neural pushforward approximation to make the problem tractable. The paper does not discuss how the approximation error in $f_\phi$ or the duality gap affects the metric axioms or the topological equivalence guarantees. If the approximated distance loses triangle inequality or symmetry, the theoretical motivation for using it as a loss function weakens. A rigorous analysis bounding the deviation of the dual or approximated distance from the primal metric would strengthen the theoretical foundation. Alternatively, the authors should clarify which theoretical properties are preserved under their specific implementation choices.

- Reliance on heuristic grid search for the regularization parameter: Section 4 states that $\lambda_C$ is selected from a discrete set $\{1, 10, 100, 1000\}$ by minimizing validation Wasserstein-2 or FID scores. The conclusion explicitly lists finding the optimal choice of $\lambda_C$ as a future direction. This heavy dependence on validation-driven grid search limits the method's practical utility, especially in settings where validation data is scarce or expensive to generate. The relationship between $\lambda_C$ and the geometric constraint $C$ in Definition 1 remains implicit, and the paper provides no principled rule for mapping data characteristics to a starting $\lambda_C$. The authors should either derive a data-driven initialization for $\lambda_C$ or provide a sensitivity analysis showing how performance degrades across a wider range of values, demonstrating robustness to suboptimal tuning.

- Unaccounted computational overhead from direction distribution learning: The performance plots in Figures 2 and 3 attribute computational efficiency primarily to the number of projections $N$ and the $O(k \log k)$ complexity of 1D Wasserstein computation. However, the proposed method requires training $f_\phi$ to optimize the direction distribution, which introduces additional gradient computations and memory overhead per iteration. The speed comparisons do not isolate the cost of learning $\sigma$ from the cost of evaluating the distance for a fixed $\sigma$. This makes it unclear whether the observed speed advantages hold when comparing methods with equivalent total training time. The authors should report the wall-clock time specifically for optimizing the direction distribution and include a computational budget comparison that accounts for the full training pipeline, not just the distance evaluation step.

- Limited discussion of linear projection limitations in high dimensions: The introduction and literature context acknowledge that linear Radon transforms lose topological information and struggle with highly curved manifolds. DSW retains this linear projection framework, with only a brief mention of the generalized version in Section 3.2 and Appendix C. The paper does not analyze how DSW behaves when the data distribution lies on a low-dimensional manifold that is poorly captured by linear slices. Without addressing this limitation, the claim that DSW balances exploration and informativeness lacks context regarding when the approach might fail. The authors should add a discussion or experiment demonstrating DSW's behavior on data with complex topological structures, or explicitly state the conditions under which linear slicing remains sufficient.

- Missing analytical computation for a parametric special case: The paper defines DSW as an optimization over probability measures on the sphere but never computes the distance value for a fully specified parametric model. Figure 1 illustrates the shape of the optimal measure for two bivariate Gaussians, but it does not report the resulting DSW value or compare it to SW and Max-SW numerically. Readers cannot calibrate how the regularization parameter C quantitatively affects the distance metric itself. Computing equation (1) for a concrete family, such as two 1D or 2D Gaussian distributions with known optimal projection directions, would let readers verify the theoretical bounds in Theorem 2 and understand how DSW interpolates between SW and Max-SW. A simple table or plot showing DSW, SW, and Max-SW values across varying covariance structures would ground the abstract optimization in tangible numbers.

- Lack of controlled synthetic experiment to isolate DSW advantage: The claim that DSW balances exploration and informativeness is tested only on large-scale image datasets, where many confounding factors exist. The introduction explicitly criticizes SW for drawing irrelevant directions when data lies on a low-dimensional manifold, yet the paper provides no synthetic example demonstrating this phenomenon. A controlled simulation with a known DGP, such as two distributions that differ only along a specific low-dimensional subspace or exhibit a specific anisotropic structure, would show exactly when and how DSW outperforms SW and Max-SW. Reporting the projection distribution and the resulting distance values on such a synthetic DGP would directly validate the core theoretical motivation.

- Absent discussion on gradient estimation and variance in GAN training: The paper applies DSW as a training objective in GANs and claims it improves convergence speed, but it omits how the gradient of the DSW loss is computed and stabilized. Equation (2) involves expectations over the uniform sphere, approximated via Monte Carlo sampling of directions. The paper does not discuss the variance of this gradient estimator, the number of samples required for stable backpropagation, or how the gradient flows through the neural pushforward map. Stable GAN training requires reliable gradient signals, yet the authors provide no guidance on sample sizes or variance reduction for the direction distribution. Providing a brief analysis of the gradient estimator's variance or a discussion of reparameterization techniques would bridge the gap between the theoretical formulation and practical implementation.

- No numerical verification that DSW recovers Max-SW in limit: The authors state that removing the regularization recovers max-(generalized) SW as a special case. Figure 1 shows the optimal measure collapsing to a Dirac delta when the regularization is removed, but the paper never verifies that the computed DSW value actually converges to the Max-SW value in this limit. A direct numerical comparison on a simple test case, such as two discrete distributions or low-dimensional Gaussians, showing that the proposed distance matches Max-SW numerically would confirm the theoretical claim. Without this verification, readers cannot be sure that the neural approximation or the Monte Carlo sampling does not introduce a bias that prevents exact recovery of the baseline method.

Recommendation: Major revision. The theoretical-practical gap, undefined optimization dynamics, and unaccounted computational costs are significant for a methodology paper claiming efficiency and metric properties. The paper needs concrete clarifications on training dynamics, approximation guarantees, and fair computational comparisons before it can be accepted.

Revision targets:

- Specify the exact update schedule for the direction distribution network $f_\phi$ during GAN training and provide an ablation study on how this schedule affects convergence and stability.

- Analyze how the neural approximation of the pushforward measure and the Lagrangian dual form affect the metric properties and topological equivalence guarantees stated in Theorems 1 and 2.

- Provide a principled initialization or data-driven rule for selecting $\lambda_C$, along with a sensitivity analysis demonstrating performance robustness across a wider range of values.

- Isolate and report the computational overhead of optimizing the direction distribution in Figures 2 and 3, ensuring speed comparisons account for the full training pipeline rather than just distance evaluation.

- Add a discussion or empirical analysis on how DSW performs when linear projections fail to capture data topology, clarifying the method's scope and limitations in high-dimensional settings.

comments.title: Reversed inequality in dual bound derivation
comments.quote: By the Lagrangian duality theory, $\text{DSW}_{p}(\mu,\nu;C)\geq\text{DSW}^{*}_{p}(\mu,\nu;C)$ for any $p\geq 1$ and admissible $C>0$.
comments.explanation: The inequality direction is mathematically reversed. For the constrained maximization defining the primal DSW, any feasible $\sigma$ satisfies the constraint $\mathbb{E}[|\theta^\top \theta'|] \leq C$. This makes the penalty term $-\lambda_C \mathbb{E}[|\theta^\top \theta'|] + \lambda_C C$ non-negative. Consequently, the penalized objective evaluated at any feasible $\sigma$ is greater than or equal to the original objective. Taking the supremum over the entire space on the right yields $\text{DSW}_{p}(\mu,\nu;C) \leq \text{DSW}^{*}_{p}(\mu,\nu;C)$. The claimed lower bound contradicts this basic relaxation argument. Correct the inequality to reflect that the dual form provides an upper bound on the primal distance.

comments.title: Incorrect geometric domain for Radon transform
comments.quote: The Radon transform (RT) maps a function $I\in\mathbb{L}^{1}(\mathbb{R}^{d})$ to the space of functions defined over space of lines in $\mathbb{R}^{d}$.
comments.explanation: The Radon transform integrates over hyperplanes (affine subspaces of codimension 1), not lines. The provided integral formula explicitly enforces the constraint $\langle x,\theta\rangle = t$, which geometrically defines a hyperplane in $\mathbb{R}^d$. Referring to the codomain as the 'space of lines' is incorrect and contradicts the delta-function formulation. Rewrite 'space of lines' as 'space of hyperplanes' to align with standard tomographic definitions and the mathematical expression provided.

comments.title: Inconsistent domain definition for direction parameter $\theta$
comments.quote: Here, $\Omega_{\theta}$ is a compact subset of $\mathbb{R}^{d}$ and $g:\mathbb{R}^{d}\times\mathbb{S}^{d-1}\mapsto\mathbb{R}$ is a defining function
comments.explanation: There is a direct domain mismatch for the direction parameter $\theta$. The text states that $\theta \in \Omega_{\theta}$ and explicitly defines $\Omega_{\theta}$ as a compact subset of $\mathbb{R}^{d}$. However, the defining function $g$ is subsequently specified to take its second argument from $\mathbb{S}^{d-1}$. This creates a type inconsistency in the composition $g(x,\theta)$. Given the context of projection directions and the standard Radon transform, $\Omega_{\theta}$ should reside on the unit sphere. Change 'compact subset of $\mathbb{R}^{d}$' to 'compact subset of $\mathbb{S}^{d-1}$' to resolve the domain conflict.

comments.title: Ill-defined measure on the GSW parameter space $\Omega_{\theta}$
comments.quote: However, these integrals are usually intractable. Thus, they are often approximated by using Monte Carlo scheme to draw uniform samples $\{\theta_{i}\}_{i=1}^{N}$ from $\mathbb{S}^{d-1}$ and $\Omega_{\theta}$.
comments.explanation: The notation $d\theta$ implies a specific measure, but $\Omega_{\theta}$ is only described as a compact set without specifying the underlying measure. Unlike the sphere $\mathbb{S}^{d-1}$, where a uniform probability measure is standard convention, a generic compact set requires an explicit measure (e.g., Lebesgue, Gaussian, or uniform on a specific bounding box) to make the integral well-defined. Furthermore, claiming uniform sampling over an unspecified compact set is mathematically ambiguous. Add a precise definition of the measure on $\Omega_{\theta}$ and justify why uniform sampling is appropriate for that specific domain.

comments.title: Unjustified convergence of stochastic ascent
comments.quote: Note that, we can use the fixed point from the stochastic ascent algorithm to approximate the dual value of DSW in equation (2).
comments.explanation: The optimization problem involves maximizing an expectation over a function class $\mathcal{F}$, which typically results in a non-concave landscape analogous to adversarial training. Stochastic gradient ascent is not generally guaranteed to converge to the global supremum or even a stationary point without strong assumptions (e.g., convex-concave structure, specific step-size rules, or minimax convergence theorems). The paper cites Appendix B.2 for a detailed argument, but this claim requires explicit justification in the main text or a reference to a convergence theorem for non-convex stochastic approximation. Add a precise convergence guarantee or cite a standard result for stochastic gradient methods in this setting to support the approximation claim.

comments.title: FID evaluation contradicts cited methodology and dataset scale
comments.quote: The FID score is calculated from 10000 random generated images and all training samples using precomputed statistics in (Heusel et al., 2017).
comments.explanation: The original FID paper (Heusel et al., 2017) explicitly states that statistics are computed from 50,000 real images, not all training samples. For LSUN, which contains 3 million images, computing FID against the entire training set is computationally prohibitive and directly contradicts the cited reference. The authors should clarify whether they subsampled the real images to 50,000 as in the standard protocol, or specify the exact subset used, and correct the text to align with the cited methodology.

comments.title: Contradictory description of feature function training
comments.quote: When working with high dimensional distributions, Deshpande et al. (2018) proposed a trick to improve the quality of the generator by learning a feature function which maps data to a new feature space that is more manageable in size. When the feature function is fixed, the generator is trained to match the distribution of features.
comments.explanation: The description contradicts itself and misrepresents the cited SWGAN method. Deshpande et al. (2018) explicitly uses a fixed feature extractor to avoid training instability. The text first claims the method involves learning a feature function, then immediately describes fixing it. This inconsistency confuses the reader about whether the feature map is trainable. Rewrite the passage to clarify that a fixed feature extractor is used, consistent with the SWGAN protocol, and remove the claim that the feature function is learned during training.

comments.title: Incorrect claim about Max-SW computational cost
comments.quote: It is interesting to note that on these high-dimensional datasets, Max-SW performs rather poorly: it obtains the highest FID scores among all distances while requires heavy computation.
comments.explanation: This claim contradicts the standard motivation for Max-SW, which is widely cited as computationally cheaper than SW because it evaluates only a single projection direction. Unless your implementation involves an unusually expensive optimization loop to find the maximum direction, stating that Max-SW requires heavy computation is misleading. Readers familiar with sliced-Wasserstein literature will expect Max-SW to be faster per iteration. Clarify the computational cost of your Max-SW implementation, or correct the statement to reflect that its primary drawback is poor sample efficiency rather than computational expense.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of Subspace Sliced-Wasserstein complexity
comments.quote: However, this approach is computationally expensive, since it could not exploit the closed-form of the one-dimensional Wasserstein distance.
comments.explanation: This claim inaccurately describes Paty and Cuturi (2019). The Subspace Sliced Wasserstein method explicitly does exploit the closed-form 1D Wasserstein distance along the optimized subspace directions. The computational bottleneck arises from optimizing the subspace itself (e.g., via SVD or Riemannian optimization), not from the distance evaluation step. Rewrite the sentence to clarify that the expense stems from subspace selection rather than a loss of closed-form evaluation.

comments.title: Imprecise description of optimization domain
comments.quote: it can be computed efficiently by searching for the optimal push-forward measure over a set of probability measures over the unit sphere satisfying certain regularizing constraints that favor distinct directions.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'optimal push-forward measure over a set of probability measures' conflates two distinct mathematical objects. A push-forward measure is uniquely determined by a single reference measure and a measurable map, whereas a 'set of probability measures' implies a space over which one searches directly. The optimization should be clarified as either searching over the space of probability measures or over a parameterized family of push-forward maps from a fixed reference distribution. Rewrite 'searching for the optimal push-forward measure over a set of probability measures' as 'searching for an optimal probability measure (or its push-forward representation) over the unit sphere' to accurately reflect the optimization domain.

comments.title: Incorrect terminology for Lp space definition
comments.quote: or any $p\geq 1$, $\mathbb{L}^{p}(\mathbb{R}^{d})$ is the set of real-valued functions on $\mathbb{R}^{d}$ with finite $p$-th moment.
comments.explanation: The notation $\mathbb{L}^p(\mathbb{R}^d)$ conventionally denotes functions with a finite $p$-th integral (i.e., $\int |f(x)|^p dx < \infty$), not a finite $p$-th moment. Moments are defined for probability measures, which the paper correctly handles using $\mathcal{P}_p(\mathbb{R}^d)$ in Section 2.1. Using 'moment' here conflates function space norms with measure-theoretic concepts. Change 'p-th moment' to 'p-th integral' to align with standard functional analysis notation.

comments.title: Missing normalization constants in SW and GSW definitions
comments.quote:
The sliced-Wasserstein distance (SW) between two probability measures $\mu$ and $
u$ is defined as *(Bonneel et al., 2015)*: $SW_{p}(\mu,
u):=(\int_{\mathbb{S}^{d-1}}W_{p}^{p} ig{(}\mathcal{R}I_{\mu}(\cdot,	heta),\mathcal{R}I_{
u}(\cdot,	heta) ig{)}d	heta)^{1/p}$.
comments.explanation: The integral definitions omit normalization factors. The Monte Carlo approximation $\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}W_{p}^{p}(\cdot)$ converges to the expectation $\mathbb{E}_{\theta}[W_{p}^{p}]$, which requires dividing the Lebesgue integral by the domain's measure (e.g., $|\mathbb{S}^{d-1}|$). Without this factor, the defined distance and its estimator differ by a constant scaling factor. Rewrite the integral definitions to explicitly include the normalization constants $\frac{1}{|\mathbb{S}^{d-1}|}$ and $\frac{1}{|\Omega_{\theta}|}$, or explicitly state that $d\theta$ denotes the uniform probability measure on the respective domains to align the definitions with the Monte Carlo estimators.

comments.title: Imprecise claim regarding uniform sampling and SW distance
comments.quote: However, important directions are not distributed uniformly over the sphere. Thus, this approach will draw potentially many unimportant projections that are not only expensive but also greatly reduce the effect of the SW distance.
comments.explanation: The statement that uniform sampling 'greatly reduce[s] the effect of the SW distance' is mathematically inaccurate. The theoretical SW distance is a fixed functional of $\mu$ and $\nu$; sampling strategy does not alter its value. Uniform sampling only affects the estimator's variance, convergence rate, and computational efficiency. The authors likely mean that uniform sampling yields high-variance estimates or inefficiently covers directions that dominate the Wasserstein distance. Rewrite the claim to specify that uniform sampling increases estimator variance or reduces sampling efficiency, rather than altering the distance itself.

comments.title: Conflation of penalty parameter and constraint bound
comments.quote: When $\lambda_{C}$ is large, $\mathbb{E}_{\theta,\theta^{\prime}\sim\sigma}\!\left[|\theta^{\top}\theta^{\prime}|\right]$ needs to be small, meaning that $C$ is small.
comments.explanation: The parameter $C$ is introduced in Definition 2 as a fixed admissible constant that defines the feasible set $\mathbb{M}_C$, not as a variable determined by the optimization. The text incorrectly suggests that tuning the Lagrange multiplier $\lambda_C$ directly sets the value of $C$. In standard Lagrangian duality, $\lambda_C$ controls the trade-off between the objective and the constraint violation, but the optimal constraint value depends on the specific distributions $\mu$ and $\nu$. Clarify whether $C$ is a user-specified hyperparameter or the resulting constraint value, and remove the implication that $\lambda_C$ directly determines $C$. Add a sentence explaining that $\lambda_C$ acts as a regularization weight that penalizes correlation, while $C$ remains a fixed bound in the primal formulation.

comments.title: Ambiguous pronoun reference in hyperparameter selection sentence
comments.quote: Finally, for  $\lambda_C$  in DSW (see Definition 2), it is chosen in the set  $\{1,10,100,1000\}$  such that its Wasserstein-2 (FID score) (between 10000 random generated images and all images from corresponding validation set) is the lowest among the four values.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'its Wasserstein-2 (FID score)' incorrectly attaches the possessive pronoun 'its' to $\lambda_C$, which is a scalar hyperparameter and does not possess a distance or score. The sentence should clarify that the resulting distance or score is minimized. Rewriting this to explicitly state that the validation Wasserstein-2 or FID score is minimized across the four candidate values will prevent reader confusion.

comments.title: Contradictory claim about DSW convergence ranking
comments.quote: Figure 2(b) shows that DSW learns better models at a faster speed of convergence than other baseline distances with a very small number of projections, e.g., DSW-10 is the second lowest curve compared to curves from other sliced-based distances.
comments.explanation: The opening sentence asserts that DSW converges faster and yields better models than all other baseline distances. The following clause immediately contradicts this by stating DSW-10 is the 'second lowest curve,' implying one baseline outperforms it. This logical mismatch weakens the reported advantage. Clarify which baseline achieves the lowest curve in Figure 2(b) and adjust the opening claim to accurately reflect that DSW is either the best or second-best performer.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of regularization mechanism
comments.quote: Our main idea is to search for the best distribution of important directions while regularizing towards orthogonal directions.
comments.explanation: The regularization term in Definition 1 bounds the expected absolute inner product, which encourages the direction distribution to be spread out or incoherent across the sphere. In high-dimensional spaces, enforcing strict orthogonality is infeasible and does not match the mathematical formulation. The phrase 'regularizing towards orthogonal directions' mischaracterizes the mechanism. Change this to 'regularizing towards spread-out directions' or 'encouraging directional diversity' to accurately reflect the incoherence constraint.
